cataloging_subject.label: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume II: Sermons on the Ferial Gospels and Sunday Epistles; Treatises
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloging unit is Thomas Arnold's 1871 volume-two selection: 116 Middle English sermons on the ferial Gospels, 55 sermons on the Sunday Epistles, Vae Octuplex, and Of Mynystris in þe Chirche. The two sermon groups belong to the larger 294-sermon English Wycliffite cycle; the two tracts are related pendants rather than sermons in that cycle. This edition-defined aggregate is distinct from each medieval component work, every manuscript witness, Arnold's printed edition, the University of Michigan encoding, and the checked-in XML. Arnold's title page, notices, contents, prefaces, marginal analyses, and notes are editorial apparatus and are not cataloged as medieval component works.

catalog_summary.title: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume II
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Wycliffite author or authors
catalog_summary.date: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; Arnold selection published 1871
catalog_summary.region: England; Wycliffite or Lollard textual culture

titles.value: Select English Works of John Wyclif, Volume II
notes: This is the edition title of the aggregate represented by CME00030, not an original medieval title for a single work.

titles.value: Sermons on the Ferial Gospels and Sunday Epistles; Treatises

notes: Arnold explains that the title derives from Laud Misc. 314's rubric for the eight woes against scribes, Pharisees, and hypocrites.

agents.name: John Wyclif
notes: Named as author by Arnold and in some medieval or early cataloging evidence, but modern scholarship does not accept sole authorship of the 294-sermon cycle as established.

agents.name: Thomas Arnold
notes: Editor of the three-volume Select English Works; he based the text on Bodley 788, supplied punctuation and other reading aids, and recorded selected variants from other manuscripts.

scope.description: Traditional attribution of the sermon cycle and its associated tracts as represented by Arnold's volume

assertion.notes: Supported as an attribution in the reception and manuscript tradition, not confirmed as a statement of authorship.

scope.description: The two sections of the unified English Wycliffite sermon cycle selected for Arnold's volume II

notes: The unity of the long cycle is textually significant, but current evidence does not resolve whether one author or collaborators composed it.

scope.description: The two associated Matthew 23 and Matthew 24 expositions

notes: The tracts are related to Wyclif's Latin expositions but are not translations of them, and their authorship remains unproved.

scope.description: The 1871 volume selection, edited texts, supplied punctuation and divisions, marginal analyses, notes, and selected apparatus
Edition transcription founded on Bodley 788, with a portion of the initial copy prepared by W. F. Cornish and the remainder by William Sorell

notes: This responsibility describes the edition layer and does not imply that Arnold authored the medieval texts.

scope.description: Selected variants cited under sigla E, G, H, I, Q, and C; Arnold says many other manuscripts were examined only cursorily

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century

assertion.notes: The range is deliberately broad because Wyclif's sole authorship and Arnold's narrower dating to Wyclif's final years are not established.

date_statements.display: Late fourteenth or early fifteenth century; Of Mynystris reflects the Great Western Schism after 1378
notes: The schism references constrain Of Mynystris but do not establish an exact year or transfer that date automatically to Vae Octuplex.

scope.description: First publication of Arnold's volume-two edited selection

places.label: Wycliffite or Lollard textual culture
notes: A cultural association rather than a claim for a precise place of composition.
Witness production places and dialects are not silently transferred to the intellectual aggregate.

scope.description: Biblical lemmata, liturgical headings, citations, and short phrases embedded in the Middle English prose

form_statements.description: All 171 sermons and both tracts are prose. The XML's 13 L elements belong to modern editorial quotations in a preface or notes and do not make the medieval selection mixed-form.

summaries.value: The volume joins two large sections of the English Wycliffite sermon cycle with two polemical biblical expositions. The ferial Gospel sermons and Sunday Epistle sermons explain the readings of the church year in Middle English while repeatedly applying scripture to belief, pastoral practice, clerical conduct, property, religious orders, and reform. Vae Octuplex applies Matthew 23's eight woes to friars and prelates. Of Mynystris in þe Chirche expounds Matthew 24 through an account of true ministry, papal wealth and pride, Antichrist, schism, persecution, and the trials of the church.

The record describes an edition-defined aggregate because CME00030 reproduces Arnold's complete volume II rather than one autonomous medieval work. The 171 sermons remain parts of the larger unified 294-sermon cycle, while Vae Octuplex and Of Mynystris are associated tracts. Modern scholarship supersedes Arnold's confident Wyclif attribution with anonymous and possibly collaborative authorship, without erasing the historical attribution.

extent.description: The medieval selection comprises 116 ferial Gospel sermons, 55 Sunday Epistle sermons, and two prose tracts. The complete XML has 15 DIV1, 175 DIV2, 2 DIV3, 356 HEAD, 638 P, 2 LG, 13 L, 1,387 NOTE, and 442 PB elements. Both LG elements and all 13 L elements represent modern editorial quotations in a preface or notes, so the medieval forms remain prose.

content_parts.label: Sermons on the Ferial Gospels
content_parts.summary: One hundred sixteen sermons, numbered CXXIV-CCXXXIX in Arnold's continuous Gospel sequence, on weekday and special-occasion Gospel readings across the liturgical year.

content_parts.label: Sermons on the Sunday Epistles
content_parts.summary: Fifty-five sermons on the Sunday Epistle readings, ordered through the church year from the first Sunday after Trinity to the final Sunday after Trinity.

content_parts.label: Vae Octuplex
content_parts.summary: An exposition of Matthew 23 that redirects Christ's eight woes against scribes, Pharisees, and hypocrites toward friars and prelates.

content_parts.label: Of Mynystris in þe Chirche
content_parts.summary: A polemical exposition of Matthew 24 on true ministry, the papacy as Antichrist, church property and poverty, persecution, schism, and the church's final trials.

related_works.title: English Wycliffite Sermons (the 294-sermon long cycle)
notes: The two sermon sections in CME00030 are later divisions of this unified cycle, not a complete representation of it.

related_works.title: John Wyclif's Latin expositions of Matthew 23 and Matthew 24
notes: Hudson identifies relationships between the English tracts and the Latin expositions but explicitly does not treat the English works as translations.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME/source/CME_phase_3/CME00030.xml, at CME submodule commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.summary: Primary local evidence for the CME identifier, complete encoded volume title and contents, Middle English and embedded Latin text, part boundaries, sermon numbering, printed-page milestones, editorial apparatus, and documented review date.

evidence.citation: Structural inspection of CME00030.xml with XPath and element counts, part-boundary review, repository hashing, and git-object identification.
evidence.locator: counts and boundaries for DIV1, DIV2, DIV3, HEAD, P, LG, L, NOTE, and PB; sermon N attributes; four medieval content divisions
evidence.summary: Finds 116 ferial Gospel sermons, 55 Sunday Epistle sermons, two prose tracts, and complete edition coverage. It distinguishes 13 lines of modern editorial quotation from the prose forms of the medieval components.

evidence.citation: Select English Works of John Wyclif, ed. Thomas Arnold, vol. II, Sermons on the Ferial Gospels and Sunday Epistles; Treatises (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1871).
evidence.locator: title page, Notice and contents, printed pp. 1-423
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for the selection title, editor, 1871 publication, complete four-part contents, sermon sequence and pagination, tract titles, Arnold's historical attribution, prefaces, and edited medieval text.

evidence.citation: Thomas Arnold, introduction and manuscript account in Select English Works of John Wyclif, vol. I (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1869), especially pp. i-xxi.
evidence.locator: introduction pp. i-xxi, especially manuscript list and editorial method pp. xvii-xxi
evidence.summary: Editor-authored evidence for the historical Wyclif attribution, sermon-cycle divisions, manuscript sigla, Bodley 788 base text, limited examination of many witnesses, supplied capitalization, paragraphing and punctuation, orthographic policy, marginal analysis, and transcription labor.

evidence.citation: Anne Hudson, 'Some Aspects of Lollard Book Production,' Studies in Church History 9 (1972), 147-158.
evidence.summary: Modern scholarly evidence that the sermon cycle must be considered as a unit; that rubric, date, and manuscript evidence do not establish Wyclif's sole authorship; that Arnold printed Bodley 788 and examined most other manuscripts only cursorily; and that Vae Octuplex and Of Mynystris are related to but not translations of Wyclif's Latin Matthew expositions.

evidence.citation: Kantik Ghosh, 'English Wycliffite Sermons: Thinking in Alternatives,' in Wycliffite Heresy: Authority and the Interpretation of Texts (Cambridge University Press, 2002).
evidence.summary: Current scholarly framing of the 294-sermon long cycle as a unified body whose single or multiple authorship remains obscure and whose scale makes collaborative production plausible.

evidence.citation: Middle English Compendium, bibliography entry BIB3873, English Wycliffite Sermons, ed. Anne Hudson and Pamela Gradon, 5 vols. (1983-1996).
evidence.summary: Current bibliographic and witness evidence for the modern five-volume edition, surviving manuscripts, witness dates, shelfmarks, and the distinction between the later scholarly base and Arnold's nineteenth-century edition.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00030.
evidence.summary: Linked evidence for the distinct manuscript, edition, facsimile, digital-encoding, and repository-artifact layers; qualified source relations; exact cache inventory; access negatives and request routes; and component-specific rights statements.

open_questions.question: What authorship or collaboration model best explains the unified 294-sermon English Wycliffite cycle, and what relationship did John Wyclif have to its composition?
next_steps: Use the Hudson-Gradon five-volume edition and later scholarship rather than Arnold's attribution as the critical starting point.
Separate evidence for cycle unity from evidence for one author, multiple authors, revision, or compilation.
Preserve manuscript attributions as reception evidence without converting them into confirmed authorship.

open_questions.question: Can the sermon groups and each associated tract be dated more narrowly without relying on Arnold's assumption of Wyclif's authorship?
next_steps: Review dating arguments in the complete modern English Wycliffite Sermons edition and later studies.
Evaluate internal Great Schism references in Of Mynystris separately from Vae Octuplex and the sermon cycle.
Do not use witness copying dates as automatic composition dates.

open_questions.question: Were Vae Octuplex and Of Mynystris composed as pendants to the sermon cycle, attached during transmission, or associated through a more complex shared textual program?
next_steps: Compare placement, rubrics, textual states, and codicological boundaries across all known tract witnesses.
Collate the English tracts with their related Latin Matthew 23 and 24 expositions without assuming translation.
Keep Arnold's description of the tracts as pendants distinct from demonstrated medieval compilation history.

notes: TEXT/@LANG='enm' describes the medieval text but not Arnold's modern English apparatus or embedded Latin lemmata.
The XML's title-page attribution to John Wyclif is edition-derived and is not treated as proof of medieval authorship.
The record remains partial because authorship, narrower composition dates, tract-cycle relations, and several source-witness access questions remain unresolved.
